topic: Someone receives flowers with no card attached
raw_output:
[story 1]

The box sat on the chipped mahogany table, an incongruous splash of vibrant color against the muted gray of my apartment. Inside, the roses were impossibly perfect, their petals velvety and deep crimson, accompanied by a sprig of lilies that smelled overwhelmingly sweet. There was no note. Just the blooms. I picked up a single rose, the stem cool against my fingertips, and my jaw tightened. Every instinct screamed at me to throw the whole arrangement into the bin, to make a scene, to erase this sudden, unwelcome intrusion. I paced the narrow confines of the living room, the silence amplifying the frantic drumming in my chest. Why? Who sends these? The thought spun endlessly, a vicious, tight knot in my stomach. I stared at the flowers, feeling a rising heat behind my eyes, a desperate urge to shatter something fragile before it could break me.

[story 2]

When the delivery man placed the bouquet on my doorstep, the chill of the late afternoon air seemed to seep right into my bones. They were exotic, too rich for my usual sparse tastes—deep purple orchids intertwined with stark white calla lilies. I hesitated, my hand hovering over the wrapping paper. A card would offer some context, some explanation for this unsolicited gesture, but the absence of writing felt like a deliberate snub. I took a deep, shaky breath, the scent of the flowers filling my nostrils, and then I simply let the box drop onto the worn welcome mat. My shoulders slumped, a heavy, defeated motion. I sank onto the floor, pulling my knees to my chest, focusing intently on the texture of the carpet fibers, trying to anchor myself against the sudden, overwhelming feeling of being watched, of being profoundly unsettled by this silent offering.

[story 3]

I found the flowers tucked beside my laptop, abandoned on the kitchen counter. They were sunflowers, aggressively cheerful, their yellow petals radiating an almost blinding light in the dim room. I didn't touch them immediately. Instead, I walked to the window, pressing my forehead against the cool glass, trying to regulate the rapid flutter of my pulse. Each breath felt shallow, insufficient. The silence of the apartment was suddenly oppressive, thick with unspoken questions. I circled the counter twice, my movements jerky and uncoordinated, my gaze darting from the bright yellow blooms to the empty space where the sender must have stood. A low, frustrated sound escaped my throat—a soft, almost inaudible sigh of pure exasperation. I snatched the bouquet, the stems feeling alien and heavy in my hand, and shoved them carelessly into a drawer, needing to contain the unexpected surge of feeling before it consumed everything.
